data.title: 買い物リスト
data.text: I always write a grocery list before going to the store. Without it, I buy too much or forget what I need. My list is simple: I write it on my phone, organized by section. Last week I forgot my list and spent twice as much on things I didn't need. The list is boring, but boring things that save money are my favorite kind.

data.title: 一文の力
data.text: The most important sentence in any email is the first one. Not because it's the most informative, but because it's the only one guaranteed to be read. People decide in seconds whether to continue, reply, or archive. A first sentence that states what you need and why it matters gets replies. Front-load the action. The backstory can wait.

data.title: 2分ルール
data.text: If something takes less than two minutes, do it right now instead of writing it down for later. Writing the task takes 30 seconds. Finding it again takes another 30. The task itself takes two minutes. Total cost of postponing: over three minutes of overhead for a two-minute job. Over a year, the rule saves hours and the anxiety of a growing list.

data.title: 名前を間違える
data.text: At a dinner party I called someone by the wrong name three times. Each time she politely corrected me. By the fourth time I was so embarrassed I avoided her for the rest of the evening. She told me later that the avoidance hurt more than the wrong name. A small mistake fixed with humor costs almost nothing, but avoiding the person costs a relationship.

data.title: 週次レビュー
data.text: Every Sunday evening I spend twenty minutes reviewing my week. Three questions, written by hand: What went well? What didn't? What will I do differently? The value isn't in any single review but in the accumulation: fifty-two reviews a year means fifty-two chances to notice patterns and catch bad habits early. Most people reflect only when something goes wrong. Reviewing when things go right teaches you what to protect.
